history_text: The patient is a 58-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed eight years ago, currently managed with metformin 1000mg twice daily. He presented to the emergency department with a two-day history of worsening chest tightness, radiating to the left arm, associated with mild breathlessness on exertion. He has a family history of ischemic heart disease, his father having suffered a myocardial infarction at age 60. On examination, blood pressure was 148/92 mmHg, heart rate 88 bpm, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. ECG showed non-specific ST-T wave changes in the lateral leads. Initial troponin was mildly elevated at 0.08 ng/mL, with a repeat troponin six hours later showing a rising trend to 0.15 ng/mL. He was admitted for further cardiac workup, started on dual antiplatelet therapy, a statin, and a beta-blocker, and referred for coronary angiography. He has no known drug allergies and does not smoke, but reports occasional alcohol use.

The patient is a 34-year-old female, gravida 2 para 1, currently at 32 weeks of gestation, referred for evaluation of persistently elevated blood pressure readings over the preceding two weeks, with the most recent reading at 152/98 mmHg. She reports mild frontal headaches over the past three days but denies visual disturbances, epigastric pain, or swelling beyond her baseline pregnancy-related oedema. Urinalysis performed at the referring clinic showed 2+ proteinuria. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated, delivered at term via normal vaginal delivery. She has no prior history of hypertension, and her booking blood pressure at 12 weeks was within normal limits. Fetal growth scans have been appropriate for gestational age to date, with normal amniotic fluid volume and Doppler studies. Given the constellation of new-onset hypertension and proteinuria in the third trimester, pre-eclampsia is suspected, and she is being referred urgently for maternal-fetal medicine assessment, closer blood pressure monitoring, and consideration of antihypertensive therapy.

The patient is a 71-year-old male with a longstanding history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, on home oxygen therapy for the past eighteen months, who presents with a five-day history of increased sputum production, change in sputum colour from clear to yellow-green, and worsening dyspnoea beyond his usual baseline. He denies fever or chest pain but reports increased use of his rescue inhaler, now required every two to three hours compared to his usual once or twice daily. He has had three similar exacerbations in the past year, two of which required hospital admission. His baseline forced expiratory volume in one second is 38% of predicted. On this presentation, oxygen saturation was 89% on his home oxygen setting of 2 litres per minute, subsequently improved to 93% on 4 litres. Chest examination revealed bilateral expiratory wheeze with prolonged expiratory phase. He was started on nebulised bronchodilators, a course of oral corticosteroids, and empirical antibiotics for a presumed infective exacerbation, with close monitoring of his respiratory status.

The patient is a 45-year-old female with no significant past medical history who presents with a six-week history of progressive fatigue, unintentional weight loss of approximately five kilograms, and intermittent low-grade fevers, predominantly occurring in the evenings. She also reports drenching night sweats requiring a change of bedclothes on several occasions over the past two weeks. On examination, non-tender, firm cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy was noted, with the largest node measuring approximately 2.5cm. There is no hepatosplenomegaly on abdominal examination. Initial blood work showed a mild normocytic anaemia with haemoglobin of 10.8 g/dL, a normal white cell count, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Given the combination of B symptoms and lymphadenopathy, there is clinical concern for an underlying lymphoproliferative disorder, and she has been referred urgently for excisional lymph node biopsy and haematology assessment to establish a definitive diagnosis before treatment planning can proceed.
